flashcards.q: What is force?
flashcards.a: A push, pull, or hit that can change the state of motion, direction, or shape of an object.

flashcards.q: What are the effects of force?
flashcards.a: Change speed, change direction, change shape/size (deformation), initiate or stop motion.

flashcards.q: Define balanced forces.
flashcards.a: Two or more forces of equal magnitude acting in opposite directions with zero net effect (resultant = 0).

flashcards.q: Define unbalanced forces.
flashcards.a: Forces whose resultant (vector sum) is non-zero, producing acceleration in the direction of net force.

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of force?
flashcards.a: Newton (N). 1 N = 1 kg·m/s² (force that accelerates 1 kg at 1 m/s²).

flashcards.q: State Newton's First Law of Motion.
flashcards.a: An object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless an unbalanced force acts on it.

flashcards.q: What is inertia?
flashcards.a: The natural tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion or rest.

flashcards.q: What is the measure of inertia?
flashcards.a: Mass. Greater mass means greater inertia (greater resistance to acceleration).

flashcards.q: Define momentum.
flashcards.a: The product of an object's mass and velocity (p = m·v). It represents the 'quantity of motion' an object possesses.

flashcards.q: What are the SI units of momentum?
flashcards.a: kg·m/s (kilogram-metre per second).

flashcards.q: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
flashcards.a: The rate of change of momentum of an object is proportional to the applied unbalanced force in the direction of force.

flashcards.q: What is the mathematical form of Newton's Second Law?
flashcards.a: F = m·a, where F is force (N), m is mass (kg), and a is acceleration (m/s²).

flashcards.q: What does the equation F = m·a tell us?
flashcards.a: Force is directly proportional to mass and acceleration. For constant force, greater mass produces smaller acceleration.

flashcards.q: What is impulse?
flashcards.a: The product of force and time duration (J = F·t). It equals the change in momentum (J = Δp).

flashcards.q: State the impulse-momentum theorem.
flashcards.a: F·t = Δp. Impulse equals change in momentum. Same momentum change can result from large force for short time or small force for long time.

flashcards.q: State Newton's Third Law of Motion.
flashcards.a: To every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. These forces act on different objects simultaneously.

flashcards.q: What is meant by action and reaction forces?
flashcards.a: When object A exerts force on object B (action), object B exerts equal and opposite force on A (reaction). Both are real and instantaneous.

flashcards.q: Do action and reaction forces cancel each other?
flashcards.a: No. They act on different objects, so they do not cancel. Each produces independent effects in its respective object.

flashcards.q: What determines whether action-reaction produce equal accelerations?
flashcards.a: The masses of the objects. Same force produces different accelerations (a = F/m) if masses differ. A_1 / A_2 = m_2 / m_1.

flashcards.q: Define friction.
flashcards.a: A force that opposes relative motion between two surfaces in contact. It acts opposite to the direction of motion or intended motion.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between static and kinetic friction?
flashcards.a: Static friction (stationary objects) self-adjusts up to a maximum value. Kinetic friction (moving objects) is constant and typically smaller than maximum static friction.

flashcards.q: What is net force?
flashcards.a: The vector sum of all forces acting on an object. It determines the object's acceleration by F_net = m·a.

flashcards.q: What is acceleration?
flashcards.a: The rate of change of velocity with respect to time. It is a vector quantity (SI unit: m/s²). Can result from speed change or direction change.

flashcards.q: If net force on an object is zero, what is its acceleration?
flashcards.a: Acceleration is zero. The object either remains at rest or continues with constant velocity (First Law).

flashcards.q: True or False: An object moving at constant velocity must have zero net force.
flashcards.a: True. Constant velocity means zero acceleration, which requires zero net force (from a = F_net / m).

flashcards.q: True or False: A large force always produces large acceleration.
flashcards.a: False. Acceleration depends on both force and mass (a = F/m). A large force on a very massive object may produce small acceleration.

flashcards.q: What is weight?
flashcards.a: The gravitational force on an object (W = mg). It differs from mass and depends on location (varies with g).

flashcards.q: How are mass and weight related?
flashcards.a: Weight = mass × gravitational field strength (W = mg). Mass is constant; weight varies with location.

flashcards.q: True or False: An object can have momentum without kinetic energy.
flashcards.a: False. Momentum p = mv and kinetic energy KE = (1/2)mv² both depend on velocity. Zero velocity means both are zero.

flashcards.q: What happens to momentum if velocity increases?
flashcards.a: Momentum increases proportionally (p = m·v). Doubling velocity doubles momentum for same mass.

flashcards.q: How does mass affect force required for given acceleration?
flashcards.a: Directly. Greater mass requires greater force for same acceleration (F = m·a). Double mass needs double force.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between force, time, and momentum change?
flashcards.a: F·t = Δp (impulse-momentum theorem). Longer contact time reduces force needed for same momentum change.

flashcards.q: Why do airbags reduce injury in car collisions?
flashcards.a: They increase the time over which momentum is reduced, thereby decreasing the required force (F·t = Δp). Lower force means less injury.

flashcards.q: True or False: Action and reaction forces always have same magnitude.
flashcards.a: True. By Newton's Third Law, action-reaction pairs are always equal in magnitude, regardless of object masses.

flashcards.q: Can an object move at constant velocity with applied force?
flashcards.a: Yes, if the applied force is balanced by other forces (like friction). Net force must be zero for constant velocity.

flashcards.q: What does it mean if velocity is zero at a point but acceleration is not zero?
flashcards.a: The object is changing direction. Velocity is instantaneously zero, but forces are still acting, causing acceleration (e.g., ball at peak of throw).

flashcards.q: How does acceleration relate to velocity change?
flashcards.a: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity (a = Δv/Δt). Greater acceleration means faster velocity change.

flashcards.q: True or False: Friction can be the only force acting on a moving object.
flashcards.a: True. For example, a sliding block experiencing only kinetic friction with no applied force experiences acceleration (deceleration) due to friction.

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of impulse?
flashcards.a: Newton-second (N·s) or kg·m/s. It equals the unit of momentum since impulse equals momentum change (F·t = Δp).

flashcards.q: Define net acceleration.
flashcards.a: The acceleration produced by the net force on an object. Calculated as a_net = F_net / m.

flashcards.q: True or False: A heavier object always falls faster than a lighter object.
flashcards.a: False (ignoring air resistance). Both fall with the same acceleration (g) in gravitational field. Mass doesn't affect falling acceleration.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between momentum and force direction?
flashcards.a: Momentum is in the same direction as velocity. Force in the momentum direction increases speed; opposite force decreases speed; perpendicular force changes direction.

flashcards.q: Can Newton's laws be applied to all objects?
flashcards.a: Yes, Newton's laws apply to macroscopic objects. For atomic/subatomic scales, quantum mechanics applies; at extreme speeds, relativity applies.

flashcards.q: True or False: Momentum is conserved only in collisions.
flashcards.a: False. Momentum is conserved in isolated systems (no external forces) regardless of whether collision occurs. Collisions are common examples.

flashcards.q: What is normal force?
flashcards.a: The contact force exerted by a surface perpendicular to it. For an object on a horizontal surface, normal force can equal or differ from weight depending on acceleration.

flashcards.q: How does applied force affect kinetic friction?
flashcards.a: Applied force doesn't affect kinetic friction magnitude (friction = μ_k × N). Friction depends on surface roughness and normal force, not applied force (though applied force may affect normal force).

flashcards.q: True or False: A car requires less force to maintain constant speed on level ground than to accelerate from rest.
flashcards.a: False (if friction is present). To maintain constant speed, force must equal friction (balanced). To accelerate from rest also requires overcoming friction plus providing net force for acceleration.

flashcards.q: What is meant by 'state of motion'?
flashcards.a: The complete description of an object's motion: its position, velocity, and direction. Newton's First Law says this state remains unchanged without unbalanced force.

flashcards.q: How does Newton's Third Law explain walking?
flashcards.a: You push ground backward (action); ground pushes you forward (reaction) with equal force, propelling you forward.

flashcards.q: True or False: A force can exist without an object.
flashcards.a: False. Forces are interactions between objects. Every force has an agent (object exerting it) and a target (object experiencing it).

flashcards.q: What is the difference between distance and displacement in the context of motion?
flashcards.a: Distance is total path length traveled (scalar). Displacement is straight-line change in position (vector). For curved paths, they differ.

flashcards.q: True or False: Greater momentum always means greater kinetic energy.
flashcards.a: False. Both depend on velocity, but momentum is p = mv while KE = (1/2)mv². For same mass, higher velocity means both are higher, but they're not proportional.

flashcards.q: What is the resultant of two equal forces acting at right angles?
flashcards.a: The resultant has magnitude F√2 and direction 45° from both forces (vector addition). F_resultant = √(F² + F²) = F√2.

flashcards.q: True or False: An object with zero velocity has zero acceleration.
flashcards.a: False. Zero velocity doesn't imply zero acceleration (e.g., ball at peak of throw has v = 0 but a = g). Acceleration depends on force, not velocity.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between net force and change in momentum?
flashcards.a: Net force equals the rate of change of momentum (F = dp/dt). From impulse-momentum: F·Δt = Δp.

flashcards.q: How do you find the acceleration of an object with multiple forces acting?
flashcards.a: Find the net force (vector sum of all forces), then divide by mass: a = F_net / m.

flashcards.q: True or False: Friction always opposes motion.
flashcards.a: Partially true. Friction opposes relative motion between surfaces. Its direction is opposite to relative motion or intended motion, not always opposite to applied force.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between speed and velocity?
flashcards.a: Speed is magnitude of velocity (scalar, no direction). Velocity includes direction (vector). Speed = |velocity|.

flashcards.q: True or False: A ball thrown upward has acceleration throughout its flight.
flashcards.a: True. Gravity continuously acts, producing constant downward acceleration (≈10 m/s²) throughout flight, even at the peak where velocity is momentarily zero.

flashcards.q: What does F = m·a imply about the relationship between mass and acceleration?
flashcards.a: Inverse relationship. For constant force, larger mass produces smaller acceleration. a = F/m shows acceleration ∝ 1/mass.

flashcards.q: How is the direction of acceleration related to the direction of net force?
flashcards.a: Acceleration is always in the same direction as net force. a and F_net are parallel vectors (same direction).

flashcards.q: True or False: Impulse and momentum have the same units.
flashcards.a: True. Impulse (F·t) has units N·s = kg·m/s, which equals momentum units (kg·m/s). They're equal by the impulse-momentum theorem.

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of mass?
flashcards.a: Kilogram (kg). Mass is a fundamental quantity in the SI system.

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of acceleration?
flashcards.a: Metre per second squared (m/s²). It represents change in velocity per unit time.

flashcards.q: True or False: An object can have constant speed but changing acceleration.
flashcards.a: True. Circular motion at constant speed has changing acceleration (directed toward center), demonstrating acceleration from direction change.
